'''Steven Renouf''' (born 8 June 1970) is an Australian former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played in the 1980s, 1990s and 2000s. Nicknamed 'the Pearl', he was known as one of the sport's greatest {{rlp|ce}}s.<ref>{{cite book 
|title= Great Australian Sporting Moments
|last= Roberts
|first= Michael
|year= 2008
|publisher= The Miegunyah Press
|location= Australia
|isbn= 978-0-522-85547-0
|page= 275
|url= https://books.google.com/books?id=dvWZHv2l7J0C
|access-date= 12 March 2011}}</ref> Renouf set numerous [[Brisbane Broncos Records|records]] for the [[Brisbane Broncos]] club. After spending eleven years with Brisbane, which yielded four premierships, he left Australia to play for English club [[Wigan Warriors]], where he spent two seasons before retiring. He was named in Australia's Indigenous team of the century (1908–2007).

==Playing career==

===Brisbane Broncos===
Of [[Indigenous Australians|Aboriginal]] and [[Europe|European]] descent, Renouf was born in [[Murgon, Queensland]] on 8 June 1970. A [[Murgon Mustangs]] junior, he developed his game with the [[Brisbane Broncos]] in his teen years. Renouf made his first grade [[NSWRL Premiership]] début in the [[1989 Brisbane Broncos season]]. He scored his first try for the club in [[Brisbane Broncos 1990|1990]], and the following year went on to set a new club record of four tries in a home fixture, and be the Broncos' top try scorer of the [[1991 Brisbane Broncos season]] and [[Brisbane Broncos 1992|1992]] (along with [[Allan Langer]]) seasons. In 1992, the Broncos claimed their first premiership, winning the [[Winfield Cup]] Grand Final against St. George 28–8, which was highlighted by a 98-metre try to Renouf in the second half. After [[Willie Carne]] had somehow managed to avoid being trapped in his in-goal, Broncos captain [[Allan Langer]] passed to Renouf who stepped inside [[Rex Terp]] and took off downfield with [[Michael Hancock (rugby league)|Michael Hancock]] in support no one in front of him after burning Saints fullback [[Michael Potter]] for speed. Veteran Dragons winger [[Ricky Walford]] gave chase and made ground on him as Renouf tired over the final 20 metres, but failed to stop him from scoring.<ref>[https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zqyZqF5W1K4 Steve Renouf 1992 Grand Final try]</ref>

In the weeks following the Broncos Grand Final win, Renouf travelled with the [[Australian Kangaroos|Kangaroos]] to England for the [[1989–1992 Rugby League World Cup#World Cup final|1992 World Cup final]] at the famous [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley Stadium]]. Renouf made his début for Australia in front of an international rugby league record attendance of 73,631 fans,<ref>{{cite news
| last = Hadfield
| first = Dave
| title = Australia rely on family virtues
| work = The Independent
| date = 29 September 1992
| url = https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-australia-rely-on-family-virtues-1554425.html
| access-date = 15 January 2010}}</ref> scoring the only try of the match after running off a pass from Broncos teammate [[Kevin Walters]] in the second half. Australia defeated [[Great Britain Lions|Great Britain]] 10–6 to win their sixth [[Rugby League World Cup]]. A week later, Renouf played for Brisbane in the [[1992 World Club Challenge]] against [[1991–92 Rugby Football League season|1991–92]] [[Rugby Football League|British]] champions [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] at [[Central Park (Wigan)|Central Park]] in [[Wigan]]. The Broncos capped their best year by defeating Wigan 22–8 and becoming the first Australian team to win the [[World Club Challenge]] in England.

In [[Brisbane Broncos 1997|1997]], Renouf scored 14 tries, which included a [[hat-trick]] against the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]] in the [[Brisbane Broncos 1997#Grand final|1997 Super League grand final]] on 20 September at ANZ Stadium in Brisbane's 26–8 victory.<ref>{{cite news
| title = Grand final simply super
| work = [[Illawarra Mercury]]
| pages = 26
| publisher = Fairfax Digital
| date = 21 September 1997
| url = http://newsstore.fairfax.com.au/apps/viewDocument.ac?page=1&docID=news970921_0406_5518
| access-date = 6 October 2009}}</ref> He was therefore named man-of-the-match.<ref>{{cite web|last=D'Souza|first=Miguel|title=Grand Final History|url=http://wwos.ninemsn.com.au/league/2009-grand-final/grandFinal_History.swf|work=wwos.ninemsn.com.au|publisher=[[Australian Associated Press|AAP]]|access-date=8 September 2013|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140819082626/http://wwos.ninemsn.com.au/league/2009-grand-final/grandFinal_History.swf|archive-date=19 August 2014}}</ref> No other player would score 3 tries in a Grand Final until Manly winger [[Michael Robertson (rugby league)|Michael Robertson]] did so in [[2008 NRL Grand Final|2008]].

He played centre for the [[Brisbane Broncos]] in their win at the [[1998 NRL grand final]]. Over a decade after leaving the Broncos he was still the club's all-time leading try scorer, having amassed a total of 142 tries.

===Wigan Warriors===
Renouf's Wigan Warriors début came in round four of the [[Challenge Cup]] against [[Whitehaven R.L.F.C.|Whitehaven]] at [[Central Park, Plymouth|Central Park]]. he scored two tries on début in his side's mammoth 98–4 victory.

In 2000, Renouf was awarded the [[Australian Sports Medal]] for his contribution to Australia's international standing in rugby league. Renouf played for the [[Wigan Warriors]] at centre their [[2000 Super League Grand Final]] loss against [[St Helens R.F.C.]] His final game for the Wigan Warriors and indeed his last game at the highest level was the [[2001 Super League Grand Final]] on 13 October at [[Old Trafford (football)|Old Trafford]] in [[Manchester]]. His side lost to the [[Bradford Bulls]] by 37–6.

Upon his time at Wigan Warriors, Renouf quoted "The two years at Wigan were absolutely brilliant. I would have liked to stay but they didn’t want to retain me"<ref name="Wigan Warriors Profile">[http://www.wiganwarriors.com/SquadMember.asp?teamid=5&id=187 "Wigan Warriors – Steve Renouf" profile], URL retrieved 11 July 2006.</ref>

===Back in Australia===
Following his return to Australia he continued playing football for [[Easts Tigers|Easts]] in the [[Queensland Cup]] alongside former Broncos teammate [[Darren Smith (rugby league)|Darren Smith]], scoring a try in the 2004 grand final loss to Burleigh.
In 2006 Renouf became the 7th former player inducted into the Broncos official Hall of Fame.<ref>{{cite web|title=Broncos Hall of Fame|url=http://www.broncos.com.au/broncos-hall-of-fame|work=broncos.com.au|publisher=[[Brisbane Broncos]]|access-date=31 December 2013|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140126143431/http://www.broncos.com.au/broncos-hall-of-fame|archive-date=26 January 2014}}</ref>
During the 2007 season at the Broncos' 20-year anniversary celebration, the club announced a list of the 20 best players to play for them to date which included Renouf.<ref>{{cite news|last=Dekroo|first=Karl|title=Still the king|work=The Courier-Mail|place=Australia|publisher=Queensland Newspapers|date=9 May 2007|url=http://www.news.com.au/couriermail/story/0,23739,21701434-10389,00.html|access-date=8 December 2009|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070512101607/http://www.news.com.au/couriermail/story/0,23739,21701434-10389,00.html|archive-date=12 May 2007}}</ref>

In August 2008, Renouf was named at centre in the ''Indigenous Team of the Century''.<ref>{{cite news|publisher=ABC News|title=Modern stars join greats in Indigenous Team of Century|url=http://www.abc.net.au/news/stories/2008/08/09/2329969.htm|archive-url=https://archive.today/20120717235608/http://www.abc.net.au/news/stories/2008/08/09/2329969.htm|url-status=dead|archive-date=17 July 2012|access-date=10 August 2008|date=9 August 2008}}</ref>

=== Hall of Fame ===
In August 2024, the [[National Rugby League]] announced that Renouf was an inductee into the [[National Rugby League Hall of Fame]]. Renouf, who was ascribed Hall of Fame number 119, was amongst eleven male players in the 2024 Class.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nrl.com/news/2024/08/14/nrl-hall-of-fame-2024-class/|title=NRL Hall of Fame 2024 Class|date=14 Aug 2024|website=NRL|publisher=NRL Media Release|access-date=14 Aug 2024}}</ref>

==Personal life==
At the age of 23, six years after signing with the Brisbane Broncos, Renouf was diagnosed with [[Diabetes mellitus type 1|type 1 diabetes]]. Renouf remembers that he "had been losing weight quite rapidly, [and] was always going to the toilet and though it was the start of pre-season, [he] had no energy, [he] couldn’t lift anything in the gym".<ref name="Steve Renouf redefines victory">[http://www.diabetesnsw.com.au/about_diabetes/steverenouf.asp "Steve Renouf redefines victory" article], URL retrieved 31 March 2008.</ref> He decided to go to the doctor and found out that he had the disease. According to Renouf "diabetes is not all doom and gloom, you can live with it."<ref name="Steve Renouf redefines victory"/> His advice to young people who have diabetes is "Never think that it can stop you from achieving your goals. Just look after it, and you can achieve anything. I looked after it...I knew I had to if I wanted to keep playing football."<ref name="Steve Renouf redefines victory"/>

Renouf has an apprenticeship as an electrician and has also worked in the industry of sales and marketing.<ref name="Role Models">[http://education.qld.gov.au/students/jnt-venture/atsi/aicapp/html/rm-srenouf.html "Role Models – Steve Renouf" article], URL retrieved 11 July 2006.</ref> He has five children – four sons and one daughter. He is currently working for the Institute for Urban Indigenous Health, as an Ambassador for the Deadly Choices preventative health program.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.deadlychoices.com.au/cms/index.php/ambassadors|title=Ambassadors|last=Dowse|first=Michelle|website=www.deadlychoices.com.au|language=en-gb|access-date=18 October 2017}}</ref>

Since retirement, Renouf has worked as a sideline commentator for [[Triple M]]'s ''Blood, Sweat and Beers'' sports show.<ref>{{cite book
| last = Harms
| first = John
| title = The Pearl: Steve Renouf's Story
| publisher = [[University of Queensland]] Press
| year = 2005
| location = Australia
| pages = 3
| url = https://books.google.com/books?id=OFE2EZ7JyEkC
| isbn = 9780702235368}}
</ref>

In February 2006, a biography of Renouf was released.  Entitled ''The Pearl: Steve Renouf's Story'', the book details his career as a professional [[rugby league]] footballer, but it also covers his early life, including his family heritage and his childhood in the Queensland town of Murgon. It also discusses how, as an Aboriginal, he had to overcome prejudices from people around him.<ref>{{Cite book|title=The Pearl: Steve Renouf's Story|last=Harms|first=John|isbn=9780702243912|location=St. Lucia, Qld.|oclc=933526218|date = 9 November 2015}}</ref>

In July 2025, Renouf was charged with high-range drink driving after allegedly blowing nearly four times the legal limit. It is alleged Renouf returned a BAC of 0.197 per cent when he was taken to the Brisbane City Watchhouse for further tests. His licence was immediately suspended, and he was charged with one count of driving under the influence of liquor.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.news.com.au/sport/nrl/brisbane-broncos-icon-steve-renouf-charged-after-allegedly-driving-nearly-four-times-over-the-limit/news-story/d79c7c54fed7c831e5d710ef96d2b397/|title=Brisbane Broncos icon Steve Renouf charged after allegedly driving nearly four times over the limit|website=www.news.com.au}}</ref>
